title: How to Lose Time and Money
gist: Paul Graham argues that most fortunes are lost not through visible overspending but through bad investments, because investing bypasses the instinctive 'self-indulgence' alarms that luxury spending trips. He extends the same structure to time: the dangerous waste is not visible fun but 'fake work' that mimics virtuous behavior and evades those alarms.

insights.claim: Most fortunes are lost not through excessive expenditure on luxuries but through bad investments, because it is hard to spend a fortune without noticing while a leveraged trade can erase a million dollars instantly.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: Capital preservation risk lives on the investment/leverage side of the ledger, not the spending side; size and monitor downside on positions, not on lifestyle.
insights.quote: the way most fortunes are lost is not through excessive expenditure, but through bad investments.
insights.locator: But in fact the way most fortunes are lost

insights.claim: Spending on luxuries trips innate, possibly hard-wired alarms about self-indulgence, whereas making an investment feels like merely moving money from one asset to another and bypasses those alarms entirely.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: Recognize that the absence of a felt 'this is risky' signal during investing is a psychological blind spot, not evidence the action is safe.
insights.quote: Investing bypasses those alarms. You're not spending the money; you're just moving it from one asset to another.
insights.locator: In most people's minds, spending money on luxuries

insights.claim: Sellers exploit the missing risk-alarm by framing expensive purchases as 'an investment' to make buyers feel they are not spending.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: Treat the word 'investment' in a sales pitch as a flag that someone is deliberately disarming your spending alarm.
insights.quote: Which is why people trying to sell you expensive things say "it's an investment."
insights.locator: In most people's minds, spending money on luxuries

insights.claim: Because the alarms that prevent bad investments are learned and counterintuitive rather than innate, the only defense is to deliberately develop new alarms.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: Risk discipline must be installed as an explicit, learned process; it will not arise from gut feeling the way frugality does.
insights.quote: The solution is to develop new alarms.
insights.locator: The solution is to develop new alarms

insights.claim: The most dangerous way to lose time is not visible leisure but 'fake work' that superficially resembles real work, because it bypasses the self-indulgence alarms that idle fun would trip.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: Audit activity by output rather than by how busy or non-fun it feels; effort that looks like work but produces nothing is the costliest time leak.
insights.quote: The most dangerous way to lose time is not to spend it having fun, but to spend it doing fake work.
insights.locator: the situation with time is much the same as with money

insights.claim: Activities like dealing with email feel like work because they are done at a desk and are not fun, which is precisely why they evade the alarms that flag wasted time.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: Non-enjoyable, desk-bound busywork is the time analog of a bad investment; flag it explicitly rather than trusting that 'not fun' means productive.
insights.quote: Dealing with email, for example. You do it sitting at a desk. It's not fun. So it must be work.
insights.locator: the same alarms don't go off on the days when I get nothing done

insights.claim: Our self-indulgence instincts were calibrated for pre-industrial life, so avoiding pleasure no longer protects against the modern traps that are new behaviors mimicking virtuous ones.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: Inherited intuition systematically misreads modern risks; deliberately built rules must replace gut feel for both capital and time allocation.
insights.quote: the most dangerous traps now are new behaviors that bypass our alarms about self-indulgence by mimicking more virtuous types. And the worst thing is, they're not even fun.
insights.locator: With time, as with money, avoiding pleasure is no longer enough
